dimensions.note: No secrets stored or vaulted. Mutating: needs WRITE creds plus the StackSet admin role (documented in README/manifest; a *-readonly profile deliberately cannot run detect). Credentials come from a named profile via fromIni (g.profile) or the ambient chain (undefined). stackSetName/callAs/region/profile are non-secret config; nothing sensitive is logged (operation ids and status are non-secret identifiers).

info on operation start (naming stackset + operation id), info per non-terminal poll (status + poll N/max), and info on terminal completion (operation + status). Structured placeholders throughout; no string interpolation into the message, no secret values logged.

Fails closed on missing creds/permissions: a DetectStackSetDrift / DescribeStackSetOperation error propagates out of the facade and aborts the method (not swallowed), and an empty OperationId throws explicitly. Poll-budget exhaustion throws a descriptive message naming the stackset and the last observed status. Every throw path precedes the single writeResource, so no partial state is written. Terminal FAILED/STOPPED is a legitimate outcome and is recorded, not thrown — correct for a drift-detection trigger.

tests/ smoke suite drives runDetect through an in-memory DriftApi with an injected no-op sleep: terminal-on-Nth-poll SUCCEEDED (asserts the full operation row), terminal FAILED recorded-not-thrown, absent-timestamps -> empty-string mapping, and poll-budget-exhausted (asserts the throw message names polls + last status and that nothing is written). isoOrEmpty unit-covered for Date/string/undefined/empty. Colocated test pins manifest/version sync, the single-method (detect) mutating surface, the single operation spec, and the upgrade-chain-ends-at-model.version invariant. Re-verified the full suite (8 tests) passes after the published-surface-hygiene scrub of the stackSetName describe example.

PASS-WITH-NOTE. Not idempotent in the create-if-not-exists sense, and that is inherent: DetectStackSetDrift is an action, not a create — each run triggers a NEW AWS-side drift-detection operation. But the operation resource is keyed by the returned operationId, so re-runs write distinct rows rather than colliding on a fixed key, and there is no persistent cloud resource to orphan (drift detection is a transient AWS-side operation that AWS reaps on its own). Partial-failure safe: a throw before writeResource leaves no swamp row, and the per-poll abort signal is forwarded to both the facade and realSleep. Note: a rapid double-run will start two independent AWS drift operations against the same stackset; this is expected for a non-idempotent trigger keyed by operationId and leaves no orphaned resource.

Two CloudFormation calls: DetectStackSetDrift (returns OperationId, validated non-empty) and DescribeStackSetOperation (StackSetOperation defaulted to {} then read field-by-field with ?? fallbacks, never assuming shape). No pagination is applicable to either call. SDK client configured with maxAttempts: 8 for transient-error retry/backoff; CallAs honored on both calls.

A single CloudFormationClient is created per run and used only for the trigger + poll calls. No file handles, sockets opened directly, or temp files. The started drift-detection operation id is captured on the operation row for downstream reference. The client is not explicitly destroy()-ed here (unlike the sibling aws-context-guard try/finally pattern); minor, not a leak in the single-execution model lifecycle, so not raised as an issue.

Re-reviewed after scrubbing the stackSetName global-arg describe example from "(e.g. 'VantaOrgSetup')" — a real third-party vendor product name — to "(e.g. 'ExampleOrgBaseline')", matching the generic placeholder used by the sibling aws-stackset-audit (the two share the global-args shape). grep -rniE 'sitoo|vanta|aikido|688375758630|m@s\.com' over the whole extension dir now returns zero hits. README/tests use generic placeholders and synthetic account ids (111111111111). No real IPs, hostnames, or account ids present. Zero real infrastructure or vendor identifiers remain on the published surface.

OperationSchema is a plain z.object with no .passthrough(), keeping the operation resource CEL-queryable. Mechanical schema-write conformance: detect writes stackSetName/operationId/action/status/creationTimestamp/endTimestamp/statusReason — a 1:1 match with OperationSchema's seven fields, no placeholders (each is sourced from the AWS operation summary or echoed from the args). Public OperationRecord shape is an explicit interface, not z.infer, so deno doc --lint stays clean.
The single operation resource declares lifetime: 'infinite' and garbageCollection: 10, matching the sibling extensions' convention (last 10 retained).

Single-purpose drift-detection trigger, not a CRUD-lifecycle model. There is no AWS resource this model owns to create/update/delete; detect starts a transient AWS-side operation and records its outcome. A sync method would be meaningless — re-running detect is the only operation.

The mutating detect method carries no in-extension pre-flight skip label. This is the documented design: the safety gate (e.g. a manual_approval step ahead of detect) belongs in the composing swamp workflow, giving least-privilege per step — the detect step runs under an admin profile while the audit step runs read-only. The README states explicitly that any safety gate belongs in the workflow ahead of this step, not in the extension.

Mechanical check: a single spec (operation) written by a single method (detect), keyed by the operation id. No cross-spec or cross-method name collision is possible; each run's distinct operation id keeps rows distinct.

detect never reads back previously stored swamp state — it triggers a fresh operation and writes its outcome. There is no update/delete/sync path, so the readResource-vs-dataRepository concern does not arise.

Initial publish (2026.06.13.0). model.upgrades carries a single identity entry whose toVersion equals model.version; the colocated test asserts that invariant. upgradeAttributes is identity (old => old), correct for an initial publish with no prior stored instances to migrate. The hygiene scrub touched only the describe() text of an existing arg, not the arg shape, so no upgrade entry is implicated.
